title: Video Storage Calculator by Bitrate 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Calculate Video File Size by Bitrate & Duration
description: Calculate video file size in GB from bitrate and duration. Free, instant calculator with no sign-up required.
intro: This calculator estimates the total file size of a video recording based on its **bitrate** (in Mbps or Kbps) and **duration** (in minutes or hours). The core formula is: **File Size (MB) = Bitrate (Mbps) × Duration (seconds) ÷ 8**. It's essential for videographers, broadcasters, streaming engineers, and content creators who need to plan storage capacity before shoots, estimate upload times, or configure recording devices. A 10 Mbps video recorded for 60 minutes, for example, produces a 4.39 GB file — knowing this upfront prevents mid-shoot storage failures and helps size cloud storage budgets accurately.
keyTakeaway: **File Size (GB) = [Bitrate (Mbps) × Duration (seconds)] ÷ 8 ÷ 1024** — e.g., 50 Mbps × 3,600 s ÷ 8 ÷ 1024 = **21.97 GB** for 1 hour of 4K ProRes footage.
useCases: A wedding videographer shooting 8 hours of 4K H.264 at 100 Mbps needs ~324 GB of SD card storage — calculating this beforehand avoids running out of cards mid-ceremony.
A live sports broadcaster encoding at 15 Mbps for a 3-hour game must pre-allocate ~20.2 GB of server space for the recorded stream before the event begins.
A YouTube creator uploading 4K footage at 35 Mbps for a 20-minute video needs to know the upload will be ~5.25 GB to estimate transfer time on a 100 Mbps connection.
A surveillance system administrator configuring 16 NVR cameras at 4 Mbps each running 24/7 must calculate ~829 GB per camera per day to size the NAS correctly for a 30-day retention policy.
A podcast video editor receiving ProRes 422 HQ files at 220 Mbps for a 2-hour recording expects a ~190 GB file and needs to confirm hard drive space before accepting the project.
explanation:
## How It's Calculated

The fundamental relationship between bitrate, duration, and file size is rooted in the definition of a "bit rate" — the number of bits processed per unit of time.

```
File Size (bits)  = Bitrate (bits/second) × Duration (seconds)
File Size (bytes) = File Size (bits) ÷ 8
File Size (KB)    = File Size (bytes) ÷ 1,024
File Size (MB)    = File Size (KB)    ÷ 1,024
File Size (GB)    = File Size (MB)    ÷ 1,024

Combined shortcut:
  File Size (GB) = [Bitrate (Mbps) × Duration (s)] ÷ 8,192
```

**Worked example — 10 Mbps for 60 minutes:**
- Duration in seconds: 60 × 60 = 3,600 s
- Bits: 10,000,000 × 3,600 = 36,000,000,000 bits
- Bytes: 36,000,000,000 ÷ 8 = 4,500,000,000 bytes
- GB: 4,500,000,000 ÷ 1,073,741,824 = **4.19 GB** (binary GiB)
- Or using the shortcut: (10 × 3,600) ÷ 8,192 = **4.39 GB** (decimal GB, 1 GB = 10⁹ bytes)

> **Note on GB vs GiB:** Consumer storage (hard drives, SD cards) is marketed in decimal GB (1 GB = 1,000,000,000 bytes), while operating systems often report in binary GiB (1 GiB = 1,073,741,824 bytes). This calculator uses **decimal GB** to match what you see on packaging and cloud storage dashboards.

---

## Reference Table

Common video formats, their typical bitrates, and resulting file sizes per hour:

| Format / Codec | Typical Bitrate | File Size / 30 min | File Size / 1 hour | File Size / 8 hours |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 480p H.264 (streaming) | 1 Mbps | 0.23 GB | 0.45 GB | 3.6 GB |
| 720p H.264 (YouTube) | 5 Mbps | 1.13 GB | 2.25 GB | 18 GB |
| 1080p H.264 (standard) | 10 Mbps | 2.25 GB | 4.5 GB | 36 GB |
| 1080p H.264 (high quality) | 25 Mbps | 5.63 GB | 11.25 GB | 90 GB |
| 4K H.264 (consumer camera) | 60 Mbps | 13.5 GB | 27 GB | 216 GB |
| 4K H.265/HEVC (efficient) | 35 Mbps | 7.88 GB | 15.75 GB | 126 GB |
| 4K ProRes 422 | 706 Mbps | 159 GB | 318 GB | 2,544 GB |
| 1080p ProRes 422 HQ | 220 Mbps | 49.5 GB | 99 GB | 792 GB |
| CCTV / Surveillance (720p) | 1–4 Mbps | 0.23–0.9 GB | 0.45–1.8 GB | 3.6–14.4 GB |
| Zoom / Video Call (720p) | 1.5 Mbps | 0.34 GB | 0.68 GB | 5.4 GB |
| Netflix 4K HDR (streaming) | 15–25 Mbps | 3.4–5.6 GB | 6.75–11.25 GB | 54–90 GB |

> Bitrate values sourced from codec documentation, Apple ProRes White Paper (2022), and Netflix Tech Blog encoder specifications.

---

## Typical Cases

### Case 1: Indie Documentary — 4K H.265 @ 50 Mbps, 4 hours of raw footage
- Duration: 4 × 3,600 = 14,400 seconds
- File Size = (50 × 14,400) ÷ 8,000 = **90 GB**
- A 128 GB SD card is sufficient; a 64 GB card would not be.

### Case 2: Corporate Livestream Archive — 1080p H.264 @ 8 Mbps, 90 minutes
- Duration: 90 × 60 = 5,400 seconds
- File Size = (8 × 5,400) ÷ 8,000 = **5.4 GB**
- Fits easily on a 16 GB USB drive with room to spare.

### Case 3: Security Camera System — 16 cameras @ 4 Mbps, 30-day retention, 24/7
- Per camera per day: (4 × 86,400) ÷ 8,000 = **43.2 GB/day**
- 16 cameras × 43.2 GB × 30 days = **20,736 GB ≈ 20.7 TB**
- A NAS with 24 TB usable space (e.g., 4 × 8 TB drives in RAID 5) would be appropriate.

---

## Common Errors

1. **Confusing Mbps (megabits) with MBps (megabytes):** Bitrate is almost always expressed in **bits** per second, not bytes. Forgetting to divide by 8 inflates your estimate by 8×. Always check whether your camera spec sheet says "Mb/s" or "MB/s."

2. **Ignoring container/wrapper overhead:** Codecs like MP4, MOV, and MKV add metadata, index tables, and audio tracks. Audio alone (AAC stereo at 320 kbps) adds ~0.14 GB/hour. For short clips, overhead can be 1–5% of total file size.

3. **Using the wrong GB definition:** A 10 Mbps × 1-hour file is 4.5 GB in decimal (10⁹) but only 4.19 GiB in binary (2³⁰). When your camera says "4.5 GB remaining," it uses decimal — but Windows Explorer may show a smaller number. This discrepancy causes confusion when transferring files.

4. **Assuming Variable Bitrate (VBR) equals the maximum bitrate:** Most modern codecs (H.264, H.265, VP9) use VBR. A camera rated at "up to 100 Mbps" may average only 40–60 Mbps over a full recording. This calculator uses **constant bitrate (CBR) logic** — for VBR, use the **average** bitrate, not the peak.

5. **Not accounting for multi-angle or multi-track recordings:** A multicam setup recording 3 angles simultaneously multiplies storage by 3. Editors often forget this when estimating raw ingest storage before an edit session.

---

faq.q: What is bitrate and how does it affect video quality?
faq.a: Bitrate is the amount of data encoded per second of video, measured in Mbps (megabits per second) or Kbps. Higher bitrates preserve more visual detail but produce larger files. For reference, Netflix streams 4K HDR at 15–25 Mbps, while professional cinema cameras like the ARRI Alexa record at over 2,000 Mbps in RAW formats. The relationship is direct: doubling the bitrate doubles the file size for the same duration.

faq.q: Why does my actual file size differ from the calculated estimate?
faq.a: Three main reasons: (1) Most cameras and encoders use **Variable Bitrate (VBR)**, so the actual average may be lower than the rated peak bitrate. (2) Audio tracks, metadata, chapter markers, and container overhead add extra bytes not reflected in the video bitrate alone. (3) The binary vs. decimal GB difference — 1 binary GiB = 1.074 decimal GB — can cause a ~7% discrepancy between what your OS shows and what storage packaging advertises.

faq.q: What bitrate should I use for uploading to YouTube in 4K?
faq.a: Google's official YouTube Help documentation recommends **35–68 Mbps** for 4K (2160p) SDR content at 24–30 fps, and **53–68 Mbps** for 4K HDR. For 1080p, they recommend 8–12 Mbps. At 35 Mbps, a 10-minute 4K video will be approximately 2.63 GB before upload compression. YouTube re-encodes your file after upload, so the viewer's experience is independent of your upload file size.

faq.q: How much storage do I need for a full day of 4K wedding video?
faq.a: A typical 8-hour wedding shoot at 4K H.264 with a consumer camera (average ~60 Mbps) generates approximately **216 GB** of raw footage. If you're running a dual-camera setup, plan for **432 GB**. Professional videographers typically carry 2–3× 128 GB SD cards per camera plus a portable SSD backup drive. For a safe buffer, always provision at least 25% more than your estimate.

faq.q: Does the formula change for H.265 (HEVC) vs. H.264?
faq.a: The **file size formula is identical** — it depends only on bitrate and duration, regardless of codec. However, H.265/HEVC achieves roughly the **same visual quality as H.264 at about half the bitrate**, per NIST and industry codec benchmarks. This means a 4K H.265 file at 35 Mbps looks comparable to a 4K H.264 file at 60–70 Mbps, but takes up nearly half the storage. The codec affects *how much quality you get per bit*, not the math of bits × time = size.

faq.q: How do I calculate storage for a 24/7 security camera system?
faq.a: Use the formula: **Total Storage (GB) = Bitrate (Mbps) × 86,400 (seconds/day) ÷ 8,000 × Number of Cameras × Retention Days**. A system with 8 cameras at 4 Mbps each with 14-day retention needs: 4 × 86,400 ÷ 8,000 × 8 × 14 = **4,838.4 GB ≈ 4.84 TB** of usable storage. Always add ~20% overhead for the file system and RAID parity if applicable.

faq.q: What's the difference between video bitrate and data transfer speed?
faq.a: Video **bitrate** (e.g., 25 Mbps) measures the density of data in the video stream itself. **Data transfer speed** (e.g., your internet connection at 100 Mbps) measures how fast data moves between systems. If your upload speed is 100 Mbps and your file is 4.5 GB (36 Gbits), the theoretical upload time is 36,000 Mb ÷ 100 Mbps = 360 seconds = **6 minutes**, ignoring protocol overhead. The BLS and FCC track broadband speeds nationally, but actual speeds vary by ISP and time of day.

faq.q: Can audio bitrate significantly impact total video file size?
faq.a: For most professional use cases, audio adds a negligible percentage. AAC stereo at 320 kbps over 1 hour adds only **0.144 GB** — about 3% of a 4.5 GB 10 Mbps video file. However, for **multichannel audio** (e.g., 7.1 surround at 640 kbps) or **lossless PCM audio** (5 Mbps+), the audio track can contribute meaningfully, adding up to ~2.25 GB/hour for uncompressed 48 kHz/24-bit stereo. Most online bitrate calculators ignore audio — this one focuses on video bitrate as the dominant factor.

faq.q: What units should I enter — Mbps or Kbps?
faq.a: Enter your bitrate in whichever unit your camera, encoder, or spec sheet uses, and make sure to match it in the calculator's input selector. Consumer cameras (GoPro, iPhone, DSLR) typically report in **Mbps** (e.g., 100 Mbps for 4K). Older or lower-resolution systems (webcams, surveillance cameras, Zoom) often use **Kbps** (e.g., 1,500 Kbps = 1.5 Mbps). 1 Mbps = 1,000 Kbps. Mixing up the two without converting will produce an estimate that's off by a factor of 1,000.
